text.title: Go Error Handling Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Concrete good/bad examples for error wrapping, HTTP mapping, panic boundaries, and agent-specific pitfalls.

example.note: Only the outermost handler logs. Intermediate service/repo layers wrap and return only.

antipattern.description: Ignoring errors with the blank identifier: data, _ := json.Marshal(user).
antipattern.reason: The ignored error is the only signal that the operation failed. Downstream code operates on a zero value or partial result, producing silent data corruption.

antipattern.description: Logging and returning: log.Printf("error: %v", err); return nil, err.
antipattern.reason: Creates duplicate log/Sentry events for every layer in the call chain. Log once at the boundary; wrap-and-return in intermediate layers.

antipattern.description: Using panic for expected runtime conditions like "user not found" or "validation failed".
antipattern.reason: Panics cross goroutine boundaries only via recover. An unrecovered panic crashes the entire process. Expected failures must return errors; panics are for programmer errors only.

antipattern.description: Comparing errors with == instead of errors.Is: if err == ErrNotFound.
antipattern.reason: == fails on wrapped errors. If any caller wraps ErrNotFound before returning it, the check silently returns false, causing the wrong HTTP status or fallthrough to the default error handler.

antipattern.description: Using pkg/errors alongside stdlib errors in the same codebase (mixing Wrap + fmt.Errorf).
antipattern.reason: The two wrapping mechanisms have incompatible Unwrap chains. errors.Is traversal may short-circuit at a pkg/errors wrapped boundary. Pick one; remove the other.

antipattern.description: Defining per-entity sentinels: ErrUserNotFound, ErrOrderNotFound, ErrProductNotFound.
antipattern.reason: Sentinel proliferation bloats apperror and forces switch cases that grow with every new entity. Use ErrNotFound with the entity name in the wrapping message.

rule.statement: Panics must not cross package boundaries except in init(). Use a recovery middleware at the HTTP or gRPC server edge. Log the full stack trace but never expose it to clients.
rule.rationale: An unrecovered panic in a goroutine silently terminates only that goroutine (for gRPC streams) or crashes the process (for HTTP without recovery middleware). Recovery at the edge prevents cascading outages.
